coredata.dc:title: Tailoring surface charge distribution of carbon dots through heteroatoms for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shengliang
Chang, Qing
Lin, Kui
Yang, Jinlong
coredata.dc:description: We have developed a facile and effective method to prepare chlorine and phosphorus-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) and investigated what the roles of these heteroatoms are played in carbon dot properties. Our researches suggested that Cl and P elements incorporated in superficial carbon atoms could tailor charge distribution on the surface of CDs, facilitating more effective photogenerated charge separation. Therefore, Cl and P-functionalized CDs show much higher photocatalytic activities than Cl and P-free CDs and commercial TiO2. This work gains an insight into how to effectively tune the photogenerated charge behaviors of CDs to achieve excellent properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Surface groups
Chlorine
Phosphorus

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of Cl,P-CDs, size distribution (c) and UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (d) of Cl,P-CDs excited at the different wavelength. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) EDX of Cl,P-CDs; (b) Full XPS spectrum of Cl,P-CDs, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) P 2p, (e) Cl 2p of Cl,P-CDs; (f) FTIR spectrum of Cl,P-CDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Comparative photocatalytic activity of Cl,P-CDs, O-CDs and TiO2. Photocatalytic degradation of MB (a) and MO (b) in the presence of commercial P25 TiO2, the prepared O-CDs and Cl,P-CDs under the visible light irradiation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic illustration of the effects of surface states on photoinduced charge transfer from inner to surface (a); PL emission spectra of Cl,P-CDs and O-CDs without (Open symbol) and with (Solid symbol) DEA (b) and DNT (c) quenchers, respectively, under the same concentrations. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

DPV scans for a) oxidation and b) reduction of the Cl,P-CDs and O-CDs. c) Schematic illustration of charge migration between the electrode and the surface states of Cl,P-CDs and O-CDs, respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Tailoring surface charge distribution of carbon dots through heteroatoms for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Instrumentation Center, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have developed a facile and effective method to prepare chlorine and phosphorus-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) and investigated what the roles of these heteroatoms are played in carbon dot properties. Our researches suggested that Cl and P elements incorporated in superficial carbon atoms could tailor charge distribution on the surface of CDs, facilitating more effective photogenerated charge separation. Therefore, Cl and P-functionalized CDs show much higher photocatalytic activities than Cl and P-free CDs and commercial TiO2. This work gains an insight into how to effectively tune the photogenerated charge behaviors of CDs to achieve excellent properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been garnered much research attention because of their unique advantages [1,2].Not only can they be prepared inexpensively and on a large scale, but also offer excellent photophysical properties without incurring the burden of intrinsic toxicity or elemental scarcity [1]. Therefore, CDs are promising substitutes for the present semiconductor quantum dots with heavy metals [3]. It is increasingly hot that CDs are used for photocatalysis as an additive of the popular photocatalyst, such as TiO2, Ag3PO4 and C3N4 [2,4–7]. The roles of CDs played in the nanocomposites harvest visible light and facilitate the excited electron transfer along their interfaces, allowing charge separation and stabilization, thus generating longer-lived holes on the surface [2,4]. This is mainly ascribed to unique photoinduced electron transfer (ET) property of CDs [8]. Due to the origin of carbon dot photoluminescence (PL) from radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes that are generated probably by charge separation in CDs [3,9–12], both electron donors and acceptors can apparently scavenge these surface-confined holes and electrons, respectively, to result in their fluorescence quenching of the CDs. Accordingly, CDs could act as electron reservoir and this capacity should depend on surface states of CDs since they are closely associated with surface energy traps induced by quantum confinement effects [6,9]. However, it remains unclear till now what kinds of surface states are responsible for enhanced this capacity of electron reservoir.

To utilize energy harvested from sunlight efficiently to promote photochemical reaction, we must understand and explore the operation of surface states for improving the transfer of excitation energy in CDs. This would allow the design of surface groups or surface compositions of CDs that can direct, sort and respond to excitation energy [10,12–14]. Typically, oxygen is one of main compositions in CDs and its content ranges from 5 to 50% depending on the synthetic route [2]. These O-containing groups in CDs not only create the surface electronic states, but also they are generally interacted with the heteroatoms introduced to modulate surface energy traps of CDs [11,12,15,16]. The OH group likely makes for surface trap stabilization while the CO group could facilitate the transfer of excitation energy [16–18]. The same function with CO group has also been observed from chlorine-containing group [19,20]. Because of high electronic affinity of the N atom, the main roles of amino groups are played in promoting radiative recombination of electrons and holes trapped on the surface of CDs [21]. Moreover, it was demonstrated that this amine-induced surface states could be reinforced dramatically by the co-doped sulfur atoms, leading to higher recombination efficiency and the reduction of recombination ways [22–24]. As compared with nitrogen, the phosphorus atom has a larger atomic radius and higher donor ability. Although the P-doped CDs and P,N co-doped CDs were also synthesized by phosphoric acid as P source and applied in bioimaging [25–27], the function of P and other heteroatoms in CDs remains confusing as the photocatalyst. In this work, we firstly prepared Cl- and P-functionalized CDs (Cl,P-CDs) by a facile approach and found the effects of Cl- and P-groups on photoinduced charge transfer and separation in CDs, inducing the enhanced photocatalytic activities.

Typically, 400 mg phosphorus pentachloride was slowly added in 20 mL ethylene glycol in a glass vial under strong agitation using a bath sonicator at room temperature. Then the obtained mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated 200 °C for 5 h (Fig. S1). The dark brown suspension containing Cl,P-CDs was obtained after naturally cooling to room temperature. This brown solution was neutralized by the addition of NaOH and was then dialyzed to remove the excess salts and reagents. The solid powders of Cl,P-CDs were obtained by freeze drying for the measurement of photocatalytic activity. The other experimental measurements and material characterizations can be seen in Supporting Information.

To investigate the intrinsic crystal structure of Cl,P-CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed. TEM image shows that Cl,P-CDs are dispersed uniformly on TEM grid (Fig. 1 a) and assume a round shape with diameters ranging from 1 to 6 nm (Fig. 1c). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) observation in Fig. 1b reveals the low crystallinity of the Cl,P-CDs. The incomplete crystallization structure are commonly observed in Cl,P-CDs. Fig. 1d presents the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the Cl,P-CDs. Analogous to previous reports, Cl,P-CDs typically show strong optical absorption in the UV region, with a tail extending out into the visible range. The peak at about 220 nm is ascribed to π–π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds; the shoulder at about 310 nm is assigned to n–π∗ transition of CO bonds [19]. The Cl,P-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behaviors, which are common in fluorescent carbon nanomaterials [2]. They have optimal excitation and emission wavelength at 380 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The radiative recombination of surface-confined electron and hole is an increasingly adopted emission mechanism for CDs [3,12]. Structure defects at the surface could offer a platform for confining excitation energy [3,28], whereas energy gaps variation of the surface energy traps should be closely associated with the surface chemistry [7,12,28]. As a result, multi-PL emission sites are generated with the changes of surface characteristics and they could be responsible for excitation-dependent PL behaviors of Cl,P-CDs.

The elemental analysis by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy shows that the Cl,P-CDs are composed of C 82.36 wt%, O 11.75 wt%, Cl 2.11 wt% and P 3.78 wt% (Fig. 2 a), in which the observed Cu element is from the TEM copper grid. To further investigate the elemental compositions and Cl, P bonding modes in Cl,P-CDs, x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement were carried out. The XPS survey spectrum of Cl,P-CDs given in Fig. 2b shows five characteristic peaks at ca. 286, 63, 132, 200 and 532 eV, corresponding to the C 1s, Na 2s, P 2p, Cl 2p, O 1s, respectively [17,20,29]. It is specially noted that the presence of Na is attributed to the added NaOH for adjusting pH. The amount of Cl and P into Cl,P-CDs are Cl 2.38 wt% and P 4.27 wt%, respectively by XPS elemental analysis, close to the value determined by EDX. Furthermore, the C1s analysis reveals three fitted peaks at 285, 286.5 and 288.9 eV, which are assigned to sp2-hybridized carbon (CC and CC bonds), CX (X = O, Cl, P) bonds and OCO bond, respectively (Fig. 2c) [22]. The slight shift of carbon–carbon binding energy in Cl,P-CDs could be resulted from the effects of their incomplete crystallization structure and heteroatom incorporation. Because the binding energy of carbon with CCl, and CP bonds is very similar to that of carbon with a CO bond, the Cl 2p and P 2p spectra are given in Fig. 2 d and e, respectively, in which the PC, PO and CCl bonds located at 130.5, 132.5 and 199.9 eV can be observed clearly [20,29]. This result was also confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum, in which the characteristic vibrational absorption bands of CO, CO, CPO, COP and CCl at 1122, 1650, 1246, 1040, 690 cm−1, respectively, were observed as shown in Fig. 2f. These analysis results strongly support that Cl and P atoms are successfully incorporated into the CDs.

The introduction of Cl and P elements leads to excellent photocatalytic activity, superior to its Cl- and P-free counterpart (O-CDs), even much better than commercial photocatalyst, TiO2 (P25). It is mentioned that Cl,P-CDs are stable as the photocatalyst and exhibit the same PL emission after the photocatalytic reaction (Fig. S2). Their photocatalytic behaviors for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO) dyes under visible-light irradiation were shown in Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. Among them, the Cl,P-CDs exhibit the highest degradation rates for MB (0.054 min−1) and MO (0.0488 min−1) via a quantitative evaluation of the first-order degradation rate constant, k = ln(C/C
0 )/t [30]. In contrast, the P25 and O-CDs require much more time to decompose the same amount of MB and MO dyes. For instance, the degradation rate of O-CDs for MB (0.0056 min−1) and MO (0.0097 min−1) dyes is only 10–20% of Cl,P-CDs under the visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the degradation rate of Cl,P-CDs was also compared with only Cl-incorporated CDs (Cl-CDs) obtained on the basis of our previous reports. It can be seen from Fig. S3 that Cl,P-CDs show the higher efficiency than Cl-CDs for dye degradation. Therefore, these demonstrations clearly indicate that the photocatalytic properties of CDs can be significantly improved by tailoring surface states via heteroatoms.

Surface states exist due to the termination of lattice periodicity at the surface [19,20,31]. The unpaired electrons in the dangling bonds of surface atoms interact with each other or heteroatoms forming a new electronic state distinguished with the inner structure [20]. This surface electronic state could result from charge transfer between the inner and the surface [7,10,13]. Thus, surface charge distribution can be described by Poisson’s equation [19,20,31].
(1)

∂ 2
V
( x , y , z )
∂
z 2
= −
ρ
ε r
ε 0
where V is the electric potential of a point (x, y, z); ε r, ε
0 and ρ are the relative dielectric constant, the vacuum permittivity and the charge density, respectively. For simplicity, the equation (1) was considered as one dimension, obtaining as:
(2)
d 2
V
( x )
d
x 2
= −
ρ
ε r
ε 0

To solve equation (2), V can be obtained as,
(3)
V
( x )
= −
x 2
2
ε r
ε 0
ρ

According to above equation (3), V is a function of the x coordinates and is influenced by ρ determined by surface atomic structure and compositions [20]. When heteroatoms or groups are introduced on the surface of CDs, the new V will be likely created with the change of ρ. On the other hand, the inhomogeneous distribution of heteroatoms on the surface of CDs could result in the change of V, which is equal to the x coordinate variation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, both kinds of CDs should have different V due to different surface compositions. Basically, the V determines surface energy trap levels or the numbers of surface-confined electrons and holes. Accordingly, the transfer of excitation energy (photogenerated electrons or holes) will depend on the surface V. When V is lower than excited state of CDs, the much more electrons on the excited state will be transferred to the surface energy traps. Conversely, the much more holes on the ground state will migrate to the surface energy traps when V is higher than excited state of CDs. The increase of hole on the surface is in favor of the formation of more active hydroxyl (*OH), which makes main contribution to photodegradation dyes, by reaction with OH− and H2O [31,32]. Although the *OH could be also generated by the interaction between the hot electrons and O2, [33] a two-step process is needed as shown in Fig. 4a, thereby causing the lower degradation rate for dyes.

One of experimental evidences in support of this explanation is the PL quenching results with both the known electron acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene, (DNT, −0.9 V vs. NHE) and donor N,N-diethylaniline (DEA, 0.88 V vs. NHE) [8]. They could scavenge surface-trapped electrons or holes and disrupt to the radiative recombination on the surface of CDs. The PL quenching efficiency depends on electric potential of the quencher. If surface-trapped energy levels own their V at the surface of CDs, the ability of charge transfer between surface trap sites of CDs and the quenchers could depend on their electric potential differences. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, PL quenching efficiencies of O-CDs and Cl,P-CDs were compared using DEA and DNT, respectively, under the same conditions. It can be seen clearly that electron donor DEA is much more effective quenching for Cl,P-CDs than O-CDs, whereas electron acceptor DNT shows stronger quenching rate for O-CDs than Cl,P-CDs. This result suggests that the hole or electron number trapped on the surface of both kinds of CDs is different. The Cl,P-CDs could have a higher V than O-CDs due to more holes trapped at the surface. To further confirm this speculation, we performed electrochemical measurements via differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) [34,35].

Because of an important characteristic of electrochemical method that mainly probes the surface of the material [35], electrochemical measurement can give information on the surface trap site levels (V). Fig. 5 a and b shows the electrochemical properties of O-CDs and Cl,P-CDs, in which one oxidation and one reduction processes were presented. The potential for the oxidation of the O-CDs is 0.51 V versus Ag/AgCl while their reduction potential is −0.54 V. As for Cl,P-CDs, however, only one an reduction potential presents at −0.33 V without observing irreversible oxidation. These results could be attributed to potential differences of surface trap states from both kinds of CDs. As illustration in Fig. 5c, there are two types of charge-transfer process involving CDs: electron injection into the neutral CDs and electron extraction from the neutral CDs, namely, hole injection. Because Cl,P-CDs possess high V, the electron transfer from electrode to Cl,P-CDs may be much easier than the opposite direction. In contrast, O-CDs with low V could accept or donate electrons upon application of a sufficiently electrode potentials, leading to irreversible oxidation and reduction processes. All of the above results jointly demonstrate that the introduction of Cl and P elements in CDs create new charge distribution that cause more holes trapped on the surface, facilitating the increase of *OH number at the surface of Cl,P-CDs.

Cl,P-CDs with several nanometers have been prepared from the mixture of phosphorus pentachloride and ethylene glycol. They exhibit excellent degradation performance for organic dyes under the visible-light irradiation, which is attributed to the introduction of Cl and P elements in CDs. The Cl and P-functionalized groups could change surface charge distribution of CDs, resulting in high electric potential at the surface. Therefore, Cl,P-CDs become easier to facilitate more effective separation of photogenerated charges inside CDs, achieving higher photocatalytic activities. This work suggests that the photogenerated charge behaviors of CDs can be tuned by heteroatom incorporation at their surface.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51272301, 51172214, U1510125, 51502270), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (2014021008).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.04.078.
